coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of novel porous graphene-based sheets and design of ultrathin composite films by in-situ sealing technology
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hailiang
Wang, Yonghui
Qin, Yang
Huang, Qinglin
Chen, Kaikai
Shu, Wei
Xiao, Changfa
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we proposed a novel interfacial polymerization (IP) method for porous graphene-based sheets (PGS) with 2D structure by recombining of amino graphene quantum dots (AGQDs) at the two phases interface, and then a large-area ultrathin composite films with excellent permeability and high selectivity were prepared by in-situ sealing technology with polyamide (PA) for the first time. Adjusting the pH of AGQDs solution could promote and regulate the formation of PGS with a lamella diameter of 2 μm, an ultrathin thickness of 3.2 nm and pore size of 2.71 nm, which showed a willow-leaf-like shape with a typical 2D porous structure. Then, the PGS at the interface was sealed in-situ by PA layer generated by injecting piperazine (PIP) in the IP process to form PGS/PA composite membrane. When the PGS synthesis time and PIP concentration were reached to 60 s and 0.02 w/v% respectively, the prepared PGS/PA composite membrane exhibited the best comprehensive properties, with an ultrathin thickness of 18.6 nm, super hydrophilic surface, excellent stability and strong alkali resistance. At this condition, the permeation flux of PGS/PA composite membrane could reach 28.4 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, which was over 300% of the original PA composite membrane (9.1 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1), while the rejection of Congo red was maintained more than 99.4%, which successfully overcame the “trade-off” effect.

head.ce:title: Controllable synthesis of novel porous graphene-based sheets and design of ultrathin composite films by in-situ sealing technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we proposed a novel interfacial polymerization (IP) method for porous graphene-based sheets (PGS) with 2D structure by recombining of amino graphene quantum dots (AGQDs) at the two phases interface, and then a large-area ultrathin composite films with excellent permeability and high selectivity were prepared by in-situ sealing technology with polyamide (PA) for the first time. Adjusting the pH of AGQDs solution could promote and regulate the formation of PGS with a lamella diameter of 2 μm, an ultrathin thickness of 3.2 nm and pore size of 2.71 nm, which showed a willow-leaf-like shape with a typical 2D porous structure. Then, the PGS at the interface was sealed in-situ by PA layer generated by injecting piperazine (PIP) in the IP process to form PGS/PA composite membrane. When the PGS synthesis time and PIP concentration were reached to 60 s and 0.02 w/v% respectively, the prepared PGS/PA composite membrane exhibited the best comprehensive properties, with an ultrathin thickness of 18.6 nm, super hydrophilic surface, excellent stability and strong alkali resistance. At this condition, the permeation flux of PGS/PA composite membrane could reach 28.4 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, which was over 300% of the original PA composite membrane (9.1 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1), while the rejection of Congo red was maintained more than 99.4%, which successfully overcame the “trade-off” effect.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, the water pollution and the fresh water shortage have become a serious global challenge to be solved urgently with the impact of population growth, industrialization and climate change [1]. Therefore, converting the not directly utilized water resources into fresh water resources has become one of the effective methods to solve the water shortage problem, such as seawater, industrial and municipal wastewater. Compared with conventional purification technology (e.g. distillation, liquefaction, extraction and adsorption, etc.), membrane separation technology is more energy-saving, environmentally friendly, space-saving and continuous operation mode [2], which has developed into an important high-tech and sustainable development technology to solve the current problems of energy, resource shortage and environmental pollution. An ideal membrane needs to ensure its high selectivity, high permeability and high stability by controlling pore size, shape and surface properties [3]. However, the “trade-off” effect between permeability and selectivity of traditional polymer membrane materials has become one of the main problems restricting their efficient development and wide application. In comparison, two-dimensional (2D) materials with the thickness of single atom or a few atoms and excellent mechanical strength are considered to be ideal components for constructing ultrathin membrane with minimum transmission resistance and maximum permeability [4-8], which can be used to further improve membrane comprehensive performance in order to overcome the “trade-off” effect.

Recently, graphene-based materials with 2D structure, such as graphene (GE), graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great interest as membrane materials due to their extremely thin thickness (only carbon atom thick), excellent mechanical strength, and easily chemical modification [9-13]. Its surface has almost no friction and only one atomic thickness, which make the membrane formed by them minimize the transmission resistance and maximize the filtration efficiency [3,14]. Hence, the application of graphene-based materials in the field of membrane separation has attracted extensive attention [15-17]. In most studies, graphene-based materials were mostly used to prepare mixed matrix membrane (MMM) and 2D laminar membranes [18].

The MMM consisted of a nanofiller (solid phase) dispersed in a continuous matrix (polymer phase) and had been researched in a lot of reports [19-22]. Qin et al., [23] added GO into the aqueous solution to participate in the interfacial polymerization (IP) and prepared the new GO-polyamide (GO-PA) selective layer. The prepared GO-PA membrane presented a new-style stripe-like surface structure and a thicker, gradually looser cross-sectional structure, which improved the water permeability of the thin film composite nanofiltration. Cao et al., [19] incorporated pristine graphene oxide (pGO) and rGO nanosheets into sodium alginate (SA) matrix to fabricate two types of hybrid membranes by physical blending method. Due to the existence of additional water channels on pGO and rGO, the water flux of the prepared membrane was significantly higher than that of the original SA membrane. The integration of graphene-based materials resulted in the formation of interfacial voids between polymer chains and nanofillers. These voids provided additional channels for molecular transport, increasing the membrane permeability [24]. However, it should be noted that the graphene-based materials only played the role of nanofillers in MMM, and there was no significant difference from other nanofiller materials. The intrinsic advantages of graphene-based materials would not be utilized sufficiently in such conditions.

In order to make full use of the morphological advantages of graphene-based materials, 2D graphene-based nanosheets were more often assembled into 2D laminar membranes [18,25-27]. There were two types of transport channels in 2D laminar membranes: horizontal and vertical nanochannels. Horizontal nanochannels consisted of interlayer channels formed by two neighboring graphene-based nanosheets and could exert a size-exclusion effect through their interlayer spacing [28]. Mi et al., [29] reported a GO membrane prepared by layer by layer deposition of GO nanosheets, which was cross-linked with polydopamine coated on polysulfone carrier by 1,3,5-Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC). Crosslinking not only provided stacked GO nanosheets with necessary stability to overcome their inherent dispersion in the water environment, but also fine-tuned the charge, spacing and other functions of the GO nanosheets. For these 2D laminar membranes, there were also a few hidden issues. For instance, the thickening of 2D laminar membranes was a common method to reduce the number of defects, but it would also increase the length of the water molecules pathways which might compromise the membrane permeability. Vertical nanochannels included in-plane pores on the substrate of graphene-based nanosheets [30-33]. However, there was no pore structure on the complete defect-free graphene-based sheets. For building additional vertical channels in graphene materials, Yang et al., [34] used chemical vapor deposition (CVD) to grow single-layer GE on Cu foil and drilled pores in the GE using O2 plasma exposure to finally prepare a large-area graphene-nanomesh/single-walled carbon nanotube (GNM/SWNT) hybrid membrane. The obtained GNM/SWNT membrane showed high water flux and high rejection ratio for salt ions or organic molecules, and maintained stable separation performance in the tubular module. Despite the great promise of porous graphene-based sheets (PGS), there was still a long way to go to extend the practical application of graphene-based materials, which required the manufacture of large-area graphene-based materials and drilling pores with easy and stable ways [35,36].

In this study, we proposed a novel PGS with 2D structure and its large-area ultrathin composite membrane for the first time, which included cutting graphene oxide to form amino graphene quantum dots (AGQDs), reorganizing at the two phases interface via IP and in-situ sealing with PA. Due to the unique structure of AGQDs, the IP reaction under pH adjustment only grew in the 2D interface, resulting in the formation of 2D sheet structure of PGS. Meanwhile, the introduction of TMC introduced nano-voids (pore structure) in the 2D sheets. For further application, the PGS at the interface was sealed in-situ by PA layer generated by injecting PIP into the aqueous phase solution to form PGS/PA composite membrane in the IP process. Here, PIP was selected as the aqueous monomer to seal the PGS, considering that most commercial nanofiltration membranes adopted PIP and TMC as monomers, which had been mature and applied [37-41]. The effects of aqueous phase pH and IP time on the structure and properties of the synthesized PGS were further revealed. By regulating PGS synthesis time and PIP concentration, the morphology, surface chemical properties and permeability of the PGS/PA composite membrane were also investigated.

Graphene oxide (GO), a material with a single layer ratio of more than 98%, a thickness of about 0.6–1.0 nm and a lamellar diameter of 0.5 μm, was obtained from Soochow Hengqiu Graphene Technology Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). 1,3,5-Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC) was provided by J&K Scientific Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Piperazine (PIP) was acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonia (NH4OH, 25 ∼ 28 wt%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and n-hexane were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Polyethersulfone (PES) UF membrane with pore sizes of 0.10 μm and 0.22 μm was procured from Haiyan XinDongFang Technology Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China).

The AGQDs were prepared via the nucleophilic addition reaction of ammonia chemically cleaved monolayer graphene oxide (GO). The AGQDs were synthesized by utilizing ammonia to open the epoxy group on GO, and various amino groups were introduced while breaking GO(the mechanism of which was shown in Fig. 1 ) [42]. Specifically, a GO aqueous dispersion liquid(0.1 w/v%) and ammonia were mixed in a ratio of 3:1. Then, the mixture was added to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100 mL) and reacted at 120℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through PES membranes with the pore sizes of 0.22 μm and 0.10 μm to obtain AGQDs solution. The obtained solution was removed excess ammonia to obtain a pure concentrated solution by reduced pressure distillation at 85℃. AGQDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying the concentrate and stored in the refrigerator for further use.

The PGS was prepared by typical IP between AGQDs in aqueous solution and TMC in organic solution. Firstly, 0.5 w/v% AGQDs aqueous solution with a certain pH and 0.1 w/v% TMC/n-hexane organic solution were prepared. Then, a special sand core funnel was used for the IP. We put the PES substrate on the sand core, added the aqueous solution (1.5 mL) into the funnel, and then added the organic solution (1.5 mL) to react with AGQDs for a certain time. After the reaction, the funnel valve was opened to drain the aqueous solution, and then the organic solution was sucked away with a straw. The synthesized PGS was attached to the PES substrate and its detailed preparation conditions were shown in Table 1 .

To investigate the separation performance of PGS, we prepared PGS/PA composite membranes by in-situ sealing technology. The detailed preparation conditions of composite membranes were shown in Table 2 . The preparation process was similar to PGS, the difference was that there was one more step than section 2.3. It was that 0.02 w/v% PIP aqueous solution of 1.5 mL was injected into the GQDs aqueous solution slowly after GQDs reaction for a period of time and then continued to react with TMC for 60 s to fabricate the large-area membranes. Finally, in order to improve the adhesion between the PGS/PA free-standing layer and the PES substrate, the obtained PGS/PA composite membrane was heat-treated at 45 ℃ for 10 min. The whole process for the preparation was illustrated in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diffusion experiment of AGQDs from aqueous phase to organic phase
ce:section.ce:para: To monitor the solubility and diffusivity of AGQDs from the aqueous phase to organic phase through the interface, 0.5w/v% AGQDs aqueous solution of 10 mL was poured in test tube, then 10 mL n-hexane was injected above the aqueous phase using a pipette. After 6 h, 3 mL n-hexane was taken near the interface to measure its UV–vis absorbance.

Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA), Raman spectrometer (Horiba, XploRA PLUS, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Fisher, USA) were used to analyze the surface functional groups, element content and other chemical properties of the AGQDs, PGS and PGS/PA composite membranes.

The morphologies of the synthesized AGQDs and PGS were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, H7650, Hitachi, Japan). Cold field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, S4800, Hitachi, Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Brooker Icon, USA) were used to observe the surface structure of the synthesized PGS and PGS/PA composite membranes. The surface charge and water contact angle (WCA) of the composite membranes were analyzed by the solid surface zeta potential analyzer (SURPASS-3, Anton Paar, Austria) and the contact angle meter (Kruss DSA-100, Germany), respectively. The pore size distribution of the synthesized PGS and PGS/PA composite membranes was analyzed by BET (BSD-PM2, China) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm using BJH (desorption isotherm) model.

The separation performance of the prepared composite membrane was evaluated using a lab-made cross-flow filtration system with the effective area of 0.63 cm2 at 2.0 or 6.0 bar operating pressure and room temperature. Before the test, the composite membrane needed to be preloaded at 2.0 or 6.0 bar for 0.5 h to reach a stable state. The water flux (

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of AGQDs and PGS
ce:section.ce:para: During the cutting of monolayer GO, ammonia was reacted with epoxy groups to form –NH2 and –OH by nucleophilic substitution, and thus AGQDs were prepared by ring-opening of the epoxide and the simultaneous direct bonding of a primary amine with a graphene edge as shown in Fig. 1 [42-44]. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, AGQDs showed characteristic peaks of the –NH stretching vibration at 3200–3500 cm−1 and 795 cm−1, the –NH bending vibration at 1590 cm−1, and the –CN stretching vibration at 1090 cm−1 [21,22]. Compared with GO, these new peaks confirmed the presence of abundant amino groups at the edges of AGQDs.

To further analyze the synthesis process of AGQDs, XPS analysis was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3b, the new N1s peak at 400 eV appeared in AGQDs and the C/N ratio of AGQDs was about 2.5:1, which indicated that AGQDs contained a lot of amino groups. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3c, it was obviously observed that a significant decrease in the C1s signal at 286.6 eV, which could prove the loss of epoxide caused by the nucleophilic reaction with ammonia. And this result could be supported by the appearance of the N1s signal at 399.9 eV and 401.1 eV, which was assigned to –NH2 at the edges of AGQDs [42].

Raman spectrum could provide convincing evidence for the GE fragment structure of the AGQDs. As shown in Fig. 3e, the peak at 1600 cm−1, corresponded to the characteristic peak of G-band of GO, which was related to the vibration of carbon atoms with sp2 bond in GO, while the peak at 1380 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic peak of D-band of GO, which was related to the lattice defects of carbon atoms in GO [42]. The AGQDs were also presented obviously G-band and D-band in the Raman spectrum, and the relative intensity of D/G band of AGQDs was ∼ 1.1, which was close to 1.0 of GO, indicating the still retaining graphene-based structure of AGQDs.

The morphologies of AGQDs and GO were observed by TEM in Fig. 3g and h, demonstrating the AGQDs with uniform nanodot structure and 3.4 nm average sheet size, which was different from the sheet structure of GO. Combined with the FTIR, XPS and Raman results, it could be revealed that the AGQDs were successfully prepared and were rich in amino groups, graphene-based structure and uniform in sheet size, which provided favorable conditions for the IP between AGQDs and TMC to form PGS.

Fig. 3f showed that the AGQDs in aqueous solution had a UV–vis absorption peak at 230 nm, and AGQDs dispersed in water emitted light blue fluorescence under irradiation from a 365 nm UV lamp, suggesting that no aggregation of the AGQDs occurred [42,45]. However, in the diffusion experiment of AGQDs, the final absorbance of AGQDs in n-hexane was almost 0, which meant that it was difficult for AGQDs to diffuse from aqueous phase to organic phase n-hexane under experimental conditions [46]. The causes of this phenomenon might be due to the much larger size of AGQDs compared with general aqueous phase monomers (such as PIP, MPD), which decreased the diffusion rate of AGQDs into organic phase. Moreover, too many hydrophilic groups on AGQDs (the total proportion of amino and oxygen-containing groups was about 55% from XPS in Fig. 3b) also made it difficult to diffuse to the organic phase.

As shown in the schematic diagram of PGS in Fig. 2, PGS was formed by polycondensation of amino group on AGQDs and acyl chloride on TMC. This could be confirmed by the –CO-NH- peak at 1650 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of PGS in Fig. 3a. Meanwhile, high-resolution XPS O1s and C1s spectra of the PGS from Fig. 3d could be also deconvoluted the peak of HN − C = O/O − C = O, confirming the PGS with a typical acylamide structure [47].

Fig. 4 showed the structure of PGS. Similar to other IP processes, the reaction of AGQDs and TMC was also a diffusion-governed process [37,48]. Compared with traditional monomers, AGQDs could hardly diffuse into the organic phase (Fig. 3f), which led to the IP of AGQDs and TMC being limited to the 2D interface, so that the growth of PGS was carried out in the 2D direction. As shown in Fig. 4(a-c), it was obviously seen that the number of PGS was increased significantly with the extension of IP time, but the morphology of PGS was almost unchanged. Overall, multiple slender willow-leaf-like PGS aggregated into an open folding fan shape. However, as shown in Fig. 3 (d-f), when the pH increased from 11.5 to 13.5, the morphology of PGS changed from no obvious structure to 2D structure and then to 3D stacking. IP was a diffusion-governed process, which greatly affected the structure of IP products [49]. The relative ratio of AGQDs to TMC at the interface could not be significantly changed with the progress of the IP due to the extremely low degree of diffusion of AGQDs, which led to the fact that the sheet size of PGS could not increase with the extension of the reaction time. The pH mainly affected the reaction degree and speed of IP. With the increase of pH, there were more and more OH– at the interface and absorbed the by-product HCl of IP reaction. Compared with traditional monomers, AGQDs with low reaction activity could not form an obvious structure within 180 s at lower pH (≤11.5). And the reaction speed was too fast, so that the IP of AGQDs and TMC could not be carried out orderly at the interface, and tended to form a random 3D stacking at higher pH (≥13.5).

As shown in Fig. 4g, the PGS obtained at pH = 12.5 was like a willow leaf with a length of about 2 μm and a width of about 0.5 μm, which was almost four times of the GO shown in Fig. 3h. In the measuring process of PGS thickness, the PGS was transferred to silicon wafer and scanned with the AFM. The corresponding AFM micrograph showed that the PGS thickness was about 3.2 nm from Fig. 4i. In order to further prove the porous structure of PGS, the pore size distribution of PGS was tested by BET, and the results showed that the pore size (Mode) of PGS was 2.71 nm from Fig. 4h [50,51].

The ultrathin 2D porous structure of PGS could be attributed to the unique properties of AGQDs in the IP process. The general strategy of IP included limiting some necessary building blocks (AGQDs and TMC) formed to immiscible solvents, so as to limit the reaction at the liquid/liquid interface [52-54]. The traditional aqueous monomer could diffuse to the organic phase through the interface and continue to grow in 3D direction. Nevertheless, IP in this work was only carried out in the 2D direction at the interface due to the extremely low diffusion of AGQDs in the organic phase, which generated PGS presented with an ultrathin 2D sheet structure [55]. As shown in Fig. 2, there were gaps on PGS due to the reaction of amino groups on the edge of AGQDs and acyl chloride of TMC, which could become pore structures for some molecules to pass through.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the PGS/PA composite membranes

ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra were applied to evaluate the surface functional groups presented in the PGS/PA composite membranes and the results were shown in Fig. 5 a. It illustrated that a typical PA structure with the peaks appeared at 1650 cm−1(C = O stretching vibration in –CO-NH-) and 1090 cm−1(C-N stretching vibration) was obtained in all the PGS/PA composite membranes [23]. Meanwhile, the typical acyl chloride peak (1774 cm−1, stretching vibration) of TMC disappeared, which should be caused by hydrolysis of the remaining acyl chloride groups [56,57]. These changes clearly illustrated that the PGS/PA layer had been successfully formed.

For further revealing the reaction of the in-situ sealing process, XPS analysis was performed and the result was shown in Fig. 5b. Except for C1s, O1s, N1s peaks, other characteristic peaks were hardly detected, especially the S element in PES substrate. This demonstrated that all the PGS/PA layers remained complete and defect-free during the transfer onto PES substrate process after the in-situ sealing, which could be supported by the about 8 nm detection depth of X-rays [58]. As shown in Fig. 5(c-f), higher-solution XPS C1s spectra of the PGS/PA layers could be deconvoluted into C − C, C − N, and N − C = O/O − C = O, confirming a typical PA structure [47]. Meanwhile, M2 exhibited the highest O content (16.26%), which was attributed to the hydrolysis of the remaining acyl chloride groups. Since the diffusion rate of PIP was reduced by PGS at the interface, some acyl chloride groups on TMC could not react completely with PIP, and NaOH in the aqueous phase also accelerated hydrolysis, making carboxyl accumulate on the surface of the PGS/PA layers. Although PGS was present in the same amount in M2 and P1, the higher PIP concentration of P1 reduced the hydrolysis of TMC. Moreover, the carboxyl on the surface resulted that all the PGS/PA composite membranes exhibited negative potential (≈-35 mV in pH = 7) in Zeta potential measurements (Fig. 5g).

Water contact angle could be used for characterizing the hydrophilicity of the composite membranes. As shown in Fig. 5h, all the prepared PGS/PA composite membranes had extremelyhydrophilic surfaces. The instantaneous water contact angles on the surface of the prepared composite membranes were less than 50°, and after 1 s contact, they become less than 35°, especially after 2 s contact, the composite membrane was completely wetted, and the contact angle become 0°. In addition, the water contact angle of M2 was the smallest (0 s:43.6°，1s:32.3°) among the prepared composite membranes. This result could agree with XPS analysis (Fig. 5b), and the highest O content of M2 endowed it with better hydrophilicity.

The pore size distributions of M0、M1 and M2 were displayed in Fig. 5i, while the adsorption and desorption isotherm were shown in Fig. S1. It was obviously seen that the M2 composite membrane exhibited a larger pore size (2.64 nm) and pore volume (0.0060 mL·g−1) than that of the M0 (2.43 nm, 0.0053 mL·g−1) and M1(2.44 nm, 0.0052 mL·g−1) membranes with the extension of PGS synthesis time. This indicated that the M2 composite membrane possessed loose structure at dense PA matrix through creating numerous voids. On the one hand, the source of these voids could be attributed to the influence of the reaction between PIP and TMC near PGS at the liquid/liquid interface, which made PA produce voids near PGS. On the other hand, it could be attributed to the unique ultrathin 2D pore structure of PGS, which had larger pore size (2.72 nm) and more pore volume (0.0062 mL·g−1) shown in Fig. 4h.

For further analyzing the effect of PGS on the PGS/PA composite membranes, their surface and cross-sectional morphologies were observed by FESEM. As shown in Fig. 6 , compared with the traditional PA structure with nodular, leaf-like or ridge-and-valley rough morphology, all the surfaces of M0、M1 and M2 were defect-free and smooth surface structure, which was attributed to uniform IP reaction and rapid heat dissipation to ensure the stability of the reaction interface [58]. However, P1 exhibited leaf-like morphology due to higher PIP concentrations [59]. Moreover, it could be clearly seen that the PGS remained its synthesized morphology (Fig. 6c1-2) with an open folding fan shape was aggregated by multiple slender willow-leaf-like PGS (Fig. 4a), and was sealed in-situ in M2, which illustrated that the sealed objects were hardly affected during the in-situ sealing process. However, there was no PGS on the surface of M0 and only partially incomplete PGS on the surface of M1, which indicated that the reaction activity of PIP was much higher than that of AGQDs, and the addition of PIP made it difficult for AGQDs to react with TMC again. Moreover, it was obvious that PGS/PA layers of M0、M1 and M2 were thin enough to see the pore structure of the PES substrate(Fig. 6e) except P1. This could also be more accurately obtained in the cross-sectional morphology. With the extension of PGS synthesis time, the thickness of the PGS/PA layers decreased significantly (M2＜M1＜M0:29.1 nm＜24.4 nm＜18.6 nm). It must be pointed out that the thickness of PA layer was proportional to the one-third power of the diffusivity of amine monomers, which was according to the kinetic model of forming PA nanofilms proposed by Freger [60]. PGS occupied part of the space of the interface and increased the resistance during the PIP diffusion process, thereby reducing the diffusivity of PIP to organic phase. Meanwhile, the too high PIP concentration greatly increased the diffusion rate of PIP, which resulted the P1 with particularly thick PGS/PA layer (230.8 nm).

In addition, as shown in Fig. 6g, when the PGS synthesis time was long (120 s), too much PGS seriously affected the diffusion of PIP, resulting in more defects of PGS/PA layer, which seriously reduced the separation performance of the composite membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of the PGS/PA composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The PGS/PA composite membranes were successfully fabricated by transferring the free-standing PGS/PA layer onto PES substrate and evaluated their permeability and rejection by a cross-flow filtration setup in dye and salt solution. In addition, all the composite membranes adopted the same PES substrate, which demonstrated that the difference in filtration performance of the composite membrane was mainly determined by PGS/PA layer. As exhibited in Fig. 7 a, the PGS/PA layer of P1 and M2 showed higher Congo red rejection (≥99.3%) than that of PES substrate (77.9%), but M2 showed much higher permeance (24.9 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) than that of P1 (2.9 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) under the test conditions of 6 bar and 25 ± 2 °C. The higher permeability was attributed to the ultrathin thickness of M2(18.6 nm in Fig. 6c3).

To further evaluate the effect of PGS on the separation performance of composite membranes, M0, M1 and M2 were measured by a cross-flow filtration in Congo red, Methyl orange, Na2SO4 and Congo red/Na2SO4 solution. The test conditions were set as 2 bar pressure and 25 ± 2 °C. As shown in Fig. 7b, M0, M1 and M2 showed a high Congo red rejection of over 99.3%, while M3 showed only 90.4%. This indicated that the PGS/PA layers of M0, M1 and M2 were complete and defect-free, while the PGS/PA layer of M3 existed more defects, which could be seen from the surface morphologies of the composite membrane in Fig. 6. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, M0, M1 and M2 showed lower rejection (less than 35%) of Methyl orange and SO4 2-, which was based on the size sieving effect. Especially, M2 (Mode pore size = 2.64 nm) displayed a higher Congo red rejection (99.4%, the molecule size was 2.56 nm) while a lower Methyl orange rejection (26.7%, the molecule size was 1.17 nm) and a lower SO4 2- rejection (16.7%, the molecule size was 0.379 nm). In addition, all the composite membrane exhibited a negative potential (≈-35 mV in pH = 7), which could improve the rejection of negative dye and salt. Besides, the molecular weight of the solute at the 90% rejection is defined as molecular weight cut off (MWCO) [61]. Fig. 7e showed the interception results of M2 membrane for neutral molecular polyethylene glycol with different molecular weight, the MWCO of M2 was 1404 Da, which showed the excellent rejection of Congo red by M2 membrane was completed by the cooperation between size-sieving and electric exclusion [62].

In general, M0, M1 and M2 showed excellent rejection of Congo red, but there were great differences in water permeance. With the extension of PGS synthesis time, the water permeance of composite membrane was gradually enlarged from 9.1 to 28.4 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, which might be attributed to the existence of PGS, the hydrophilicity of the surface and the difference of thickness. As shown in Fig. S2, the flux was inversely proportional to the membrane thickness. It could be seen that the flux of M2 obtained in experiment (28.1 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) was much higher than that obtained in theory (16.8 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1), which indicated that the thickness reduction was not the main reason for the increase in the flux of M2 compared with M0. Apparently, the biggest difference of M0 and M2 came from the existence of PGS, which indicated that PGS had greatly improved the water permeance of the composite membrane. Therefore, the excellent properties of PGS itself and the looser structure of the membrane when loaded into the PGS/PA separation layer were the key reasons for the improvement of membrane flux. In general, AGQDs still retained smooth and frictionless surface structures similar to graphene, which allowed water molecules to flow through the channels very rapidly [63-65]. Therefore, the surface of PGS assembled by AGQDs had very low water transmission resistance. Moreover, theoretical calculations have predicted that single-layer nanoporous 2D membranes could provide ultrafast water permeation and selective separation [66]. The nanoporous structure of PGS was conducive to the vertical transport of water molecules and could be used as a channel for ultrafast water transport [67,68]. What’s more, when water molecules passed through the PGS/PA layer, part of the 3D network channels in the pure PA layer was replaced by the ultrathin 2D channels of PGS. PGS had extremely low transmission resistance due to its ultrathin structure, which led to a significant increase in water permeance of M2 while maintaining high Congo red rejection, breaking through the “trade-off” effect between permeability and selectivity of the membrane. In addition, PGS made the PGS/PA layer form a cavity near PGS, which made the PGS/PA layer looser and more improved its water permeance [37].

Moreover, it could be seen that M2 had excellent dye/salt selectivity factor (98.7) in treating Congo red/Na2SO4 solution from Fig. 8 a. Furthermore, compared the dye/salt separation performance of PGS/PA composite membrane with other membranes reported in the literature from Fig. 8b, M2 not only presented excellent water permeance, but also had high salt/dye selectivity, which indicated that it could be applied in the treatment of saline dye wastewater based on high permeation of salt and high rejection for dyes [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and alkali resistance of the PGS/PA composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 9 a, both the water permeance and Congo red rejection of M2 remained stable during the 48 h operation. This demonstrated that the PGS/PA layer was not only defect-free and robust, but also sufficiently tightly bound to PES substrate during the test.

Moreover, in order to evaluate the strong alkali resistance of the composite membrane, the pre-pressure filtration test of M2 composite membrane was carried out at different times with NaOH solution(pH = 13) under the pressure of 2 bar, and then the membrane was washed with water to test the separation performance of Congo red solution. As shown in Fig. 9b, M2 composite membrane still maintained excellent permeability after 24 h preloading of NaOH solution, indicating that the PGS/PA layer had excellent strong alkali resistance and could be applied to the separation process in strong alkali environment.

In summary, a novel PGS was fabricated by a cutting-reorganizing method which included cutting GO to form AGQDs and then reorganizing at the two phases interface via IP. Due to the unique structure of AGQDs and regulation of pH, the IP reaction could only grow in the 2D interface, resulting in the formation of PGS with 2D sheet structure and nano-voids (pore structure). As expected, the PGS showed a willow-leaf-like shape with a typical 2D porous structure, presenting a lamella diameter of 2 μm, an ultrathin thickness of 3.2 nm and pore size of 2.71 nm. On this basis, large-area ultrathin PGS/PA composite membranes were prepared by in-situ sealing technology of injecting PIP into aqueous solution to form PA for the first time. When the PGS synthesis time and PIP concentration were reached to 60 s and 0.02 w/v% respectively, the prepared PGS/PA composite membrane had enough PGS with a complete structure and exhibited the best comprehensive properties, revealing an ultra-thin thickness of 18.6 nm, super hydrophilic surface, excellent operation stability and strong alkali resistance. At this condition, the permeation flux of PGS/PA composite membrane could reach 28.4 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, which was over 300% of the original PA composite membrane (9.1 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1), while the rejection of Congo red was maintained more than 99.4%, which successfully overcame the “trade-off” effect. The cutting-reorganizing and in-situ sealing strategy provide an innovative and convenient way for porous graphene-based materials to give full play to their unique 2D structure in the field of membrane separation.
